brick_definitions.name: action.approval
brick_definitions.description: Request human approval before continuing (Brick-First canonical name for the approval runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use when a pipeline needs a human decision before proceeding.

brick_definitions.name: action.move_file
brick_definitions.description: Move or copy a file to a new location on the local filesystem or OneDrive.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to archive processed files, organise output into folders, or stage files for downstream processing. Use operation=move to remove the source after copying.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When the source is a cloud file that needs a download first (use source.fetch_files + file_read). When you need to rename a OneDrive file without downloading it — use mcp_call with the M365 server. When bulk moving thousands of files — write a Python helper with shutil.copytree instead.

source.description: Absolute source file path (inside Brix container, e.g. /host/root/...)

destination.description: Absolute destination path (file or directory). If a directory, the filename is preserved.

operation.description: 'move' removes the source after copying; 'copy' keeps the source

overwrite.description: Whether to overwrite the destination if it already exists

examples.goal: Archive a processed PDF to the done/ folder
Copy a report to a backup location

brick_definitions.name: action.notify
brick_definitions.description: Send a notification via Mattermost, email, or log output at the end of a pipeline step.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to alert a user or channel about pipeline results, errors, or completion. Use at the end of pipelines or in error handlers.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When you need to send a full formatted email with attachments (use mcp_call with the M365 server). When the target channel does not exist yet (create it first). When you only need debug output — use log channel and the pipeline's built-in step logging instead.

message_template.description: Jinja2 message template. Use {{ steps.<id>.result }} to reference step results. Markdown is supported for mattermost.

target.description: For mattermost: channel name or user handle (e.g. '@hans' or '#general'). For email: recipient email address.

examples.goal: Notify Mattermost channel when pipeline completes

brick_definitions.name: action.respond
brick_definitions.description: Send a response back to a caller or user (Brick-First canonical name for the respond runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use as the final step in a pipeline to return a structured response.

brick_definitions.name: cody.batch
brick_definitions.description: Cody batch operation for foreach

brick_definitions.name: cody.call
brick_definitions.description: Base Cody MCP call via SSE

brick_definitions.name: cody.gatekeeper
brick_definitions.description: Cody gatekeeper review with 120s timeout

brick_definitions.name: cody.notify
brick_definitions.description: Cody notification with severity routing

brick_definitions.name: cody.read
brick_definitions.description: Cody read operation with 5min cache

brick_definitions.name: cody.task_start
brick_definitions.description: Start Cody task with saga compensation

brick_definitions.name: convert.extract_text
brick_definitions.description: Extract plain text from PDF, DOCX, or image files using native parsing or OCR.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need raw text from documents, especially scanned PDFs or images where native text extraction is unavailable. Choose method=ocr for scans.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When you need structured Markdown with headers and tables (use convert.to_markdown). When the file is already plain text. When you need to process Excel sheets (use convert.to_json with input_format=csv after export).

input_path.description: Absolute path to the input file (PDF, DOCX, PNG, JPG, TIFF)

method.description: Extraction method: 'native' for text-based PDFs/DOCX, 'ocr' for scans/images

language.description: OCR language hint (ISO 639-1 code, e.g. 'de', 'en') — only used when method=ocr

examples.goal: Extract text from a native PDF

brick_definitions.name: convert.to_json
brick_definitions.description: Parse structured data files (CSV, XML, YAML) and return them as a JSON object or list.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to load structured data from a file format into the pipeline context. Ideal for CSV exports, config files, and XML data feeds.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When the input is already JSON — just use file_read. When the input is a PDF or Word document (use convert.to_markdown first). When you need only selected columns from a large CSV (write a Python helper instead).

input_path.description: Absolute path to the input file

examples.goal: Parse a CSV export into a list of records

brick_definitions.name: convert.to_markdown
brick_definitions.description: Convert any document (PDF, DOCX, XLSX, HTML, image) to clean Markdown text via the markitdown service.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to extract readable text from a document before LLM processing or classification. Works best for PDF invoices, Word documents, Excel sheets, and scanned pages.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When the file is already plain text or Markdown. When you need structured table data from Excel (use convert.to_json instead). When the document is an image and you need OCR with spatial layout (use convert.extract_text with method=ocr).

input_path.description: Absolute path to the file to convert (inside the Brix container, e.g. /host/root/...)

examples.goal: Convert a PDF invoice to Markdown for LLM extraction

brick_definitions.name: db.ingest
brick_definitions.description: Write one or more records into a database table, with optional upsert (insert-or-update).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to persist structured data into SQLite or PostgreSQL at the end of a pipeline. Use upsert_key to avoid duplicates when re-running the same pipeline.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When you only need to write to a file (use file_write instead). When the data needs complex transformation before insert (run a transform step first). When writing to external SaaS databases via API — use mcp_call for that.

columns.description: List of column names to write. If omitted, all keys of the input records are used.

upsert_key.description: Column name to use as upsert key. If a row with this key value exists, it is updated.

connection.description: Database connection string or credential name (e.g. 'sqlite:///data.db' or 'POSTGRES_URL')

examples.goal: Insert extracted invoice data into the invoices table

brick_definitions.name: db.query
brick_definitions.description: Execute a SQL query against a database and return the results as a list of dicts.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to read existing data from a database for enrichment, deduplication checks, or reporting within a pipeline.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When you need to write data (use db.ingest instead). When the data is in a file (use file_read or convert.to_json). When you need a single lookup by ID — consider passing the value via pipeline params instead.

query.description: SQL SELECT query to execute. Use :param_name for named parameters.

connection.description: Database connection string or credential name

brick_definitions.name: db.upsert
brick_definitions.description: Upsert records into the Brix database (Brick-First canonical name for the db_upsert runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to insert or update records in the application database.

brick_definitions.name: extract.specialist
brick_definitions.description: Declarative data extraction using rules (Brick-First canonical name for the specialist runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to extract structured fields from unstructured text without Python scripts.

brick_definitions.name: file.read_base64
brick_definitions.description: Liest Datei von Disk und gibt base64 + filename + size zurueck
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Wenn eine Datei fuer einen nachfolgenden Step als base64 benoetigt wird (z.B. vor http.request Upload)
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: Wenn die Datei als Text gelesen werden soll (nutze file.read stattdessen)

brick_definitions.name: file.write_text
brick_definitions.description: Schreibt Text (z.B. Markdown) in eine Datei auf Disk
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Wenn ein Step-Ergebnis (Markdown, JSON, Text) als Datei gespeichert werden soll
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: Wenn Daten in eine DB geschrieben werden sollen (nutze db.upsert)

brick_definitions.name: file_read
brick_definitions.description: Read a file and return its content.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Loading configuration files, reading input data, accessing local files.

brick_definitions.name: file_write
brick_definitions.description: Write content to a file.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Saving results, exporting data, creating output files.

brick_definitions.name: filter
brick_definitions.description: Filter a list using a Jinja2 boolean expression. No Python script needed.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Filtering lists by condition: only PDFs, only items matching a keyword, removing empty entries.

where.description: Jinja2 expression that evaluates to true/false per item (use 'item' variable)

brick_definitions.name: flow.aggregate
brick_definitions.description: Aggregate items by group and compute statistics (Brick-First canonical name for the aggregate runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to group and summarize lists of items.

brick_definitions.name: flow.choose
brick_definitions.description: Conditional branching (Brick-First canonical name for the choose runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use when different step sequences should run depending on a condition.

brick_definitions.name: flow.dedup
brick_definitions.description: Deduplicate a list of items (Brick-First canonical name for the dedup runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to remove duplicate items from a list based on a key.

brick_definitions.name: flow.diff
brick_definitions.description: Compute the difference between two lists or datasets (Brick-First canonical name for the diff runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to find new, changed, or removed items between two snapshots.

brick_definitions.name: flow.error_handler
brick_definitions.description: Handle errors from previous steps (Brick-First canonical name for the error_handler runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to catch and handle errors in a pipeline gracefully.

brick_definitions.name: flow.filter
brick_definitions.description: Filter a list using a Jinja2 expression (Brick-First canonical name for the filter runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to filter items in a foreach or list step.

brick_definitions.name: flow.flatten
brick_definitions.description: Flatten a list of lists into a single list (Brick-First canonical name for the flatten runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use after foreach or parallel steps that produce nested lists.

brick_definitions.name: flow.merge
brick_definitions.description: Merge multiple step outputs into one (Brick-First canonical name for the merge runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use after parallel branches to consolidate their results.

brick_definitions.name: flow.parallel
brick_definitions.description: Run sub-steps in parallel (Brick-First canonical name for the parallel runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to execute independent steps concurrently.

brick_definitions.name: flow.pipeline
brick_definitions.description: Run a sub-pipeline (Brick-First canonical name for the pipeline runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to compose complex workflows from reusable pipeline building blocks.

brick_definitions.name: flow.pipeline_group
brick_definitions.description: Run multiple sub-pipelines in parallel (Brick-First canonical name for the pipeline_group runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to fan-out across multiple pipelines and collect their results.

brick_definitions.name: flow.repeat
brick_definitions.description: Repeat a sequence of steps until a condition is met (Brick-First canonical name for the repeat runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use for polling loops or iteration until convergence.

brick_definitions.name: flow.set
brick_definitions.description: Set context variables (Brick-First canonical name for the set runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to assign computed or static values to context variables.

brick_definitions.name: flow.switch
brick_definitions.description: Multi-branch switch/case control flow (Brick-First canonical name for the switch runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use when you need to route to one of several branches based on a value.

brick_definitions.name: flow.transform
brick_definitions.description: Transform data using a Jinja2 expression (Brick-First canonical name for the transform runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to reshape or remap data between pipeline steps.

brick_definitions.name: flow.validate
brick_definitions.description: Validate data quality rules (Brick-First canonical name for the validate runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to assert data integrity before proceeding.

brick_definitions.name: flow.wait
brick_definitions.description: Wait for a specified duration or condition (Brick-First canonical name for the wait runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to add delays or wait for external events between pipeline steps.

brick_definitions.name: http.request
brick_definitions.description: Make an HTTP request (Brick-First canonical name for the http runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use when you need to call a REST API or HTTP endpoint.

brick_definitions.name: http_get
brick_definitions.description: Make an HTTP GET request to a URL and return the response.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Fetching data from REST APIs, downloading JSON, polling status endpoints.

brick_definitions.name: http_post
brick_definitions.description: Make an HTTP POST request with a JSON or text body.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Sending data to REST APIs, triggering webhooks, uploading content.

brick_definitions.name: llm.batch
brick_definitions.description: Run LLM inference in batch mode (Brick-First canonical name for the llm_batch runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to classify, extract or summarize many items using an LLM in a single batch.

brick_definitions.name: llm.classify
brick_definitions.description: Use an LLM to classify text or a document into one of several predefined categories.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to sort items into categories based on content semantics: email routing, document tagging, intent detection. Best for multi-class classification where a simple keyword match is insufficient.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When the categories can be determined by regex or keyword matching (use filter or specialist). When you need to extract multiple fields — use llm.extract instead. When the classification needs a confidence score — add confidence to the output_schema of llm.extract. When you have a training dataset — consider a fine-tuned classifier instead.

prompt_template.description: Jinja2 prompt template. Use {{ input.text }} for the text and {{ config.categories | join(', ') }} for categories. Should instruct the LLM to return JSON with 'category' and 'rationale' fields.

examples.goal: Classify an email as invoice, contract, or newsletter

brick_definitions.name: llm.extract
brick_definitions.description: Use an LLM to extract structured fields from unstructured text based on a prompt template and an output schema.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to pull specific fields (amounts, dates, names, IBANs) from free-form text and regex/json_path cannot reliably handle the variation. Ideal after convert.to_markdown or convert.extract_text.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When the data is already structured JSON — use transform or filter instead. When a simple regex pattern is sufficient (use the specialist brick). When you need binary classification — use llm.classify instead. When the text is too long for a single LLM context window without chunking.

prompt_template.description: Jinja2 prompt template. Use {{ input.text }} to reference the input text. Should instruct the LLM to return JSON.

output_schema.description: JSON Schema describing the expected output fields

examples.goal: Extract invoice number, date, and total amount from invoice text

brick_definitions.name: markitdown.convert
brick_definitions.description: Convert documents to Markdown via markitdown (Brick-First canonical name for the markitdown runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use to convert PDF, DOCX, XLSX, HTML, or images to Markdown text.

brick_definitions.name: mcp.call
brick_definitions.description: Call a tool on a registered MCP server (Brick-First canonical name for the mcp runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use when you need to interact with an MCP-compatible service.

brick_definitions.name: mcp_call
brick_definitions.description: Call a tool on a registered MCP server via stdio protocol.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Interacting with M365, Docker, n8n, or any MCP-compatible service.

brick_definitions.name: python_script
brick_definitions.description: Run a Python script file. Script reads JSON from argv[1] or stdin, writes JSON to stdout.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Data transformation, filtering, file processing — anything that needs Python logic.

params.description: Parameters passed as JSON to the script

brick_definitions.name: run_cli
brick_definitions.description: Execute a shell command and return stdout. Always uses argument list (shell=False) for security.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Running system commands: ffmpeg, pandoc, docker exec, any CLI tool.

brick_definitions.name: script.cli
brick_definitions.description: Execute a CLI command (Brick-First canonical name for the cli runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use when you need to run a shell command or CLI tool.

brick_definitions.name: script.python
brick_definitions.description: Run a Python script (Brick-First canonical name for the python runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use when you need Python logic, data processing, or a custom script.

brick_definitions.name: source.fetch
brick_definitions.description: Fetch data from a source connector (Brick-First canonical name for the source runner).
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Use as the first step in a pipeline to fetch emails, files, or other source data.

brick_definitions.name: source.fetch_emails
brick_definitions.description: Fetch emails from Outlook or Gmail via MCP.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need to read emails from a mailbox at the start of a pipeline. Use as the first step when the pipeline processes email content or attachments.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When you already have the emails as input from a previous step. When you only need attachments from a known message ID (use mcp_call directly). When the pipeline receives emails via webhook/trigger.

filter.description: OData filter (Outlook) or IMAP search string (Gmail), e.g. 'isRead eq false'

limit.description: Maximum number of emails to fetch

examples.goal: Fetch unread emails from Outlook inbox
Fetch all emails from Gmail for the last week

brick_definitions.name: source.fetch_files
brick_definitions.description: Fetch files from OneDrive or a local directory, with optional glob-pattern filtering.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When you need a list of files to process (PDFs, DOCX, images) from cloud storage or disk. Use as the first step in document-processing pipelines.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When you already have file paths as input from a previous step. When you need to read file *content* directly (use file_read for that). When the source is an email attachment (use source.fetch_emails + attachment download instead).

path.description: Root path or OneDrive folder path to scan

pattern.description: Glob pattern to filter files, e.g. '*.pdf' or '**/*.docx'

examples.goal: List all PDFs in OneDrive /Dokumente folder

brick_definitions.name: source.http_fetch
brick_definitions.description: Make an HTTP GET or POST request to fetch data from an external API or webhook.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: When the data source is a REST API, web service, or webhook endpoint. Suitable for JSON APIs, paginated endpoints, and status checks.
brick_definitions.when_NOT_to_use: When the data source is a registered MCP server (use mcp_call instead — more reliable, typed). When you need to download binary files like PDFs (use file_read or a dedicated download step). When the API requires OAuth2 flows that are already wrapped in an MCP connector.

body.description: Request body (sent as JSON) — only used for POST/PUT/PATCH

examples.goal: Fetch JSON data from a public API

brick_definitions.name: specialist
brick_definitions.description: Declarative data extraction: apply regex / json_path / split / template rules to extract fields from text or structured data, validate the result, and return it in dict / list / flat format. Replaces 600-line Python helper scripts with a compact YAML config block.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Extracting structured fields from unstructured text (invoice numbers, IBANs, dates, amounts), reshaping nested JSON without a Python helper, or any declarative field extraction + validation pipeline step.

input_field.description: Dot-notation path into the pipeline context (default: 'text')

extract.description: List of extraction rules. Each rule: {name, method, pattern?, template?, default?, group?, findall?}. method: regex | json_path | split | template

checks.description: Optional validation rules. Each rule: {field, rule, value?, on_fail?}. rule: required | min_length | max_length | regex | type. on_fail: warn | skip | error

brick_definitions.name: sub_pipeline
brick_definitions.description: Run another saved pipeline as a sub-step.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Composing complex workflows from reusable pipeline building blocks.

brick_definitions.name: transform
brick_definitions.description: Transform data using a Jinja2 expression. Maps, renames, restructures.
brick_definitions.when_to_use: Renaming fields, extracting nested values, reshaping data between steps.

output_schema.description: Normalized item in the standard NormalizedItem format.

source.description: Connector name

parameters.name: top

connector_definitions.name: local_files

output_schema.description: Normalized item in the standard NormalizedItem format.

source.description: Connector name

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: Absolute path to the directory to scan.

connector_definitions.name: onedrive
connector_definitions.description: Microsoft OneDrive / SharePoint file storage via M365 MCP. Lists, downloads, and processes files and folders.

output_schema.description: Normalized item in the standard NormalizedItem format.

source.description: Connector name

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: Filename glob pattern to filter files (e.g. '*.pdf').

connector_definitions.name: outlook
connector_definitions.description: Microsoft Outlook / Exchange email via M365 MCP. Fetches, filters, and reads emails and their attachments from Outlook inboxes.

output_schema.description: Normalized item in the standard NormalizedItem format.

output_schema.description: Normalized item in the standard NormalizedItem format.

parameters.name: end_date
parameters.description: Fetch transactions up to this date (ISO 8601).

mcp_tool_schemas.name: brix__add_step
mcp_tool_schemas.description: Add a step to an existing pipeline. Use after brix__create_pipeline to build up the pipeline step by step. Supply either 'brick' (for brick-registry steps) or 'type' (for flow-control steps such as repeat, stop, choose, parallel, set, notify, approval). Exactly one of 'brick' or 'type' must be provided. All extra parameters (until, max_iterations, sequence, choices, values, when, message, etc.) are passed through as step configuration. Returns the updated pipeline with the new step appended.

step_id.description: Unique step identifier within the pipeline (e.g. 'fetch_emails').

brick.description: Brick name to use for this step (e.g. 'http_get', 'mcp_call'). Mutually exclusive with 'type'.

type.description: Direct step type for flow-control steps that are not bricks (e.g. 'repeat', 'stop', 'choose', 'parallel', 'set', 'notify', 'approval'). Mutually exclusive with 'brick'.

params.description: Step-specific parameters. May use Jinja2 templates referencing prior steps.

parallel.description: Whether to run this step in parallel with compatible siblings.

position.description: Where to insert step, e.g. 'after:<step_id>'. Default: append.

source.description: Optional caller identity — expected on every MCP call. Fields: session (pipeline/session name), model (LLM model id), agent (agent name). Used for audit logging. Example: {"session": "buddy-session", "model": "opus", "agent": "agent-alpha"}

session.description: Pipeline or session name that triggered this call.

mcp_tool_schemas.name: brix__auto_fix_step
mcp_tool_schemas.description: Attempt to automatically fix a failing step in a pipeline. Handles: ModuleNotFoundError (installs missing package), UndefinedError/is undefined (adds | default('') via update_step), Timeout (doubles the timeout value via update_step). Returns {fixed, action, rerun_hint} — use rerun_hint to re-run the pipeline.

run_id.description: Run ID of the failed pipeline run.

source.description: Optional caller identity — expected on every MCP call. Fields: session (pipeline/session name), model (LLM model id), agent (agent name). Used for audit logging. Example: {"session": "buddy-session", "model": "opus", "agent": "agent-alpha"}

session.description: Pipeline or session name that triggered this call.

mcp_tool_schemas.name: brix__cancel_run
mcp_tool_schemas.description: Cancel a running pipeline by run_id. Writes a cancel_requested.json sentinel into the run workdir so the engine and helper scripts stop at the next checkpoint. If the run is an async background run the asyncio.Task is also cancelled immediately. History is updated with status='cancelled', reason, and finished_at. Returns {success, cancelled, run_id, reason}.

source.description: Optional caller identity — expected on every MCP call. Fields: session (pipeline/session name), model (LLM model id), agent (agent name). Used for audit logging. Example: {"session": "buddy-session", "model": "opus", "agent": "agent-alpha"}

session.description: Pipeline or session name that triggered this call.

mcp_tool_schemas.name: brix__check_resource
mcp_tool_schemas.description: Check the lock status of a named resource. Expired locks are treated as free. Returns {locked: bool, run_id, claimed_at, expires_at}.

source.description: Optional caller identity — expected on every MCP call. Fields: session (pipeline/session name), model (LLM model id), agent (agent name). Used for audit logging. Example: {"session": "buddy-session", "model": "opus", "agent": "agent-alpha"}

session.description: Pipeline or session name that triggered this call.

mcp_tool_schemas.name: brix__claim_resource
mcp_tool_schemas.description: Acquire a distributed lock on a named resource. Use before starting exclusive operations (e.g. writing to a file, running a pipeline). Returns {claimed: true, expires_at} on success or {claimed: false, held_by, expires_at} on conflict. Expired locks are automatically released.

run_id.description: Identifier of the current run or agent claiming the lock.

ttl_minutes.description: Lock TTL in minutes (default: 30). Lock auto-expires after this time.

source.description: Optional caller identity — expected on every MCP call. Fields: session (pipeline/session name), model (LLM model id), agent (agent name). Used for audit logging. Example: {"session": "buddy-session", "model": "opus", "agent": "agent-alpha"}

session.description: Pipeline or session name that triggered this call.

mcp_tool_schemas.name: brix__compose_pipeline
mcp_tool_schemas.description: Intent-to-Pipeline Assembly: Describe what you want in natural language, get a pipeline proposal from existing bricks and helpers. Always use this BEFORE creating a new pipeline to maximise reuse. Analyses your goal, discovers matching bricks/helpers/pipelines, and assembles a structured step-by-step proposal with coverage report. Returns parsed_intent, matches (pipelines/helpers/bricks), proposed_pipeline steps, coverage percentage, missing steps list, and next_steps guidance.

goal.description: What the pipeline should do, described in natural language. Example: 'Download emails from Outlook and store attachments in a database.'

Once registered, pipeline steps can reference it via 'helper: <name>' instead of spelling out the full script path. Requirements listed here are auto-installed before the step runs. Returns the registered entry with name, script, and schema. Use brix__create_helper instead if you want to write the code inline.

requirements.description: PEP-508 package requirements (e.g. ['httpx>=0.28', 'pandas']). Auto-installed before the step runs.

input_schema.description: JSON Schema for the helper's accepted input parameters.

help_topics.name: anti-patterns
help_topics.title: Alle NIEMALS-Regeln mit Erklärung und richtiger Alternative.
help_topics.content:
=== Anti-Patterns — Was NIEMALS zu tun ist ===

## 1. YAML manuell schreiben

  ❌ NIEMALS: Datei in pipelines/*.yaml direkt bearbeiten
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__create_pipeline mit steps inline

  Warum: Volume-gemountet bedeutet Änderungen gehen verloren; Validierung fehlt.

## 2. brix run via Bash

  ❌ NIEMALS: Bash("brix run my-pipeline.yaml")
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__run_pipeline(name="my-pipeline")

  Warum: Kein structured output, kein run_id, keine Status-Verfolgung.

## 3. delete + recreate statt update

  ❌ NIEMALS: delete_pipeline → create_pipeline (um Steps zu ändern)
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: update_step / add_step / remove_step / update_pipeline

  Warum: Versions-Historie geht verloren; unnötiger Overhead.

## 4. base64 in foreach-Loops

  ❌ NIEMALS: {"base64": "<200KB_PDF_als_base64>", "name": "file.pdf"}
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: {"path": "/host/root/docs/file.pdf", "size": 204800}

  Warum: 80 PDFs × 200KB base64 = 16MB JSON pro Step → OOM-Crash.

## 5. concurrency als Jinja2-Template

  ❌ NIEMALS: concurrency: "{{ input.workers }}"
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: concurrency: 5

  Warum: concurrency muss zur Parse-Zeit ein int sein; Jinja2 nicht erlaubt.

## 6. Credentials als Input-Parameter

  ❌ NIEMALS: brix__run_pipeline(params={"api_key": "sk-geheim..."})
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__credential_add + {{ credentials.MY_KEY }} im Step

  Warum: Credentials landen in Logs, Run-Historie, und Output.

## 7. Container rebuilden nach Pipeline-Änderungen

  ❌ NIEMALS: docker compose build nach pipeline/helper Änderungen
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: Direkt testen — Volume-Mount macht Rebuild überflüssig

  Warum: Build dauert Minuten; pipelines/ und helpers/ sind live gemountet.

## 8. steps. Prefix in Referenzen

  ❌ NIEMALS: {{ steps.my_step.data }}
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: {{ my_step.output }}

  Warum: Brix-Syntax hat keinen 'steps.' Prefix; Feld heißt 'output'.

## 9. Teure Zwischenergebnisse nicht persistieren

  ❌ NIEMALS: LLM-Batch über 100 Dokumente ohne Checkpoint
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: Ergebnisse in state_set / Datei nach jedem Batch speichern

  Warum: Bei Crash geht alles verloren; kein Resume möglich.

## 10. foreach ohne | default([])

  ❌ NIEMALS: foreach: "{{ maybe_step.output.items }}" (wenn Step optional)
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: foreach: "{{ maybe_step.output.items | default([]) }}"

  Warum: UndefinedError wenn vorheriger Step übersprungen wurde.

## 11. Bash-Workarounds — Alle bekannten Patterns

  ❌ NIEMALS: Bash("cat ~/.brix/runs/<run_id>/...")
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__get_run_log(run_id=...) / brix__get_run_status(run_id=...)

  ❌ NIEMALS: Bash("cat ~/.brix/pipelines/<name>.yaml")
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__get_pipeline(name=...)

  ❌ NIEMALS: Bash("python3 -c 'import json; ...'")
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__create_helper + brix__run_pipeline mit python-Step

  ❌ NIEMALS: Bash("rm -f ~/.brix/...")
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__delete_run / brix__delete_pipeline / brix clean

  ❌ NIEMALS: Bash("docker exec brix-mcp brix run ...")
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__run_pipeline(name=...)

  ❌ NIEMALS: Bash("ls ~/.brix/pipelines/")
  ✅ STATTDESSEN: brix__list_pipelines()

  Warum: Bash-Workarounds umgehen Validierung, Logging, run_id-Tracking und
  strukturierten Output. Sie brechen bei Container-Neustarts oder Pfad-Änderungen.

help_topics.name: credentials
help_topics.title: Credential Store Workflow, UUID-Referenzen.
help_topics.content:
=== Credential Store ===

## Workflow

1. Credential speichern:

  brix__credential_add(
    name="OPENAI_KEY",
    value="sk-...",
    description="OpenAI API Key",
    tags=["llm", "api"]
  )

  → Gibt zurück: credential_id (UUID)

2. In Pipeline referenzieren:

  {
    "id": "call_llm",
    "type": "python",
    "config": {
      "code": "import openai; openai.api_key = credential_value"
    },
    "credentials": {"credential_value": "OPENAI_KEY"}
  }

  Oder in Jinja2:
  {{ credentials.OPENAI_KEY }}

3. Credential rotieren:

  brix__credential_rotate(name="OPENAI_KEY", new_value="sk-new...")

## Sicherheitsregeln

  ❌ NIEMALS Credentials als Pipeline-Input übergeben:
     brix__run_pipeline(params={"api_key": "sk-..."})
     → Landet in Logs und Run-Historie

  ✅ IMMER Credential-Store nutzen:
     {{ credentials.MY_KEY }} im Step-Code

## Credential-Management

  brix__credential_list()                   → Alle Credentials (ohne Werte)
  brix__credential_get(name="MY_KEY")       → Credential mit Wert
  brix__credential_update(name, value)      → Wert aktualisieren
  brix__credential_rotate(name, new_value)  → Rotieren (Versions-History)
  brix__credential_search(query="openai")   → Suchen
  brix__credential_delete(name="MY_KEY")    → Löschen

help_topics.name: debugging
help_topics.title: 4-Schritt Guide: get_run_errors → diagnose_run → auto_fix_step → get_run_log.
help_topics.content:
=== Debugging — 4-Schritt Guide ===

## Schritt 1: Fehler identifizieren

  brix__get_run_errors(run_id="<run_id>")

  → LLM-optimierte Fehleranalyse
  → Zeigt fehlgeschlagene Steps + Fehlermeldungen
  → Direkt actionable (welcher Step, was ist schiefgelaufen)

## Schritt 2: Diagnose

  brix__diagnose_run(run_id="<run_id>")

  → Schritt-für-Schritt-Diagnose des gesamten Runs
  → Zeigt Context-Variablen, Input/Output pro Step
  → Identifiziert Root-Cause bei Kaskaden-Fehlern

## Schritt 3: Auto-Fix (für häufige Fehler)

  brix__auto_fix_step(run_id="<run_id>", step_id="<step_id>")

  Automatisch behebbare Fehler:
  • ModuleNotFoundError → pip install Vorschlag
  • Timeout → timeout-Parameter erhöhen
  • UndefinedError (Jinja2) → fehlende | default() hinzufügen
  • ConnectionError → retry-Logik vorschlagen

## Schritt 4: Vollständiges Log

  brix__get_run_log(run_id="<run_id>")

  → Vollständiges stdout/stderr Log
  → Alle Step-Outputs
  → Timestamps

## Häufige Fehlertypen

  UndefinedError: 'step_id' is undefined
  → Step existiert nicht oder wurde übersprungen
  → FIX: Step-ID prüfen, | default({}) hinzufügen

  UndefinedError: 'data' is undefined
  → {{ step_id.data }} → muss {{ step_id.output }} heißen
  → FIX: .data durch .output ersetzen

  TypeError: 'NoneType' is not subscriptable
  → Step hat keinen Output (fehlgeschlagen oder übersprungen)
  → FIX: when-Kondition prüfen, | default({}) verwenden

  ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'xxx'
  → Python-Modul nicht im Container installiert
  → FIX: auto_fix_step oder pip install im python-brick

  ValidationError: concurrency must be integer
  → concurrency: '{{ input.n }}' → concurrency: 5
  → FIX: Jinja2-Template durch int-Literal ersetzen

help_topics.name: error-patterns
help_topics.title: Error-Pattern-Registry: Fehler dokumentieren, suchen, beim nächsten Mal sofort lösen.
help_topics.content:
=== Error-Patterns Registry — Fehler dokumentieren und wiederfinden ===

Die 'error_patterns' Registry erfasst bekannte Fehler mit Ursache und Fix.
Ziel: Wiederkehrende Fehler sofort erkennen und lösen — kein Debugging von Grund auf.

## Wann nutzen?

  → Nach jedem neuen Fehler den du gelöst hast: Registry-Eintrag erstellen
  → Bei Fehler in get_run_errors: registry_search(query=<error-text>)
  → Bei Auto-Fix (diagnose_run): prüft Registry automatisch

## Felder im Content-Objekt

  error_regex   (str)   — Regex-Pattern das den Fehler-String matcht
  root_cause    (str)   — Warum passiert das?
  fix           (str)   — Konkrete Lösung / Workaround
  pipeline      (str)   — Betroffene Pipeline oder 'any'
  severity      (str)   — 'low', 'medium', 'high', 'critical'

## Beispiele

### OData-Filter zu komplex
  brix__registry_add(
    registry_type="error_patterns",
    name="odata-filter-too-complex",
    description="Microsoft Graph OData-Filter führt zu 400 Bad Request",
    content={
      "error_regex": "The expression is too complex",
      "root_cause": "Microsoft Graph limitiert OData-Filter-Komplexität",
      "fix": "Filter vereinfachen: einzelne Bedingungen statt AND/OR-Ketten. Breit fetchen + lokal filtern.",
      "pipeline": "download-attachments",
      "severity": "medium"
    },
    tags=["m365", "odata", "graph"]
  )

### Python-Import fehlt
  brix__registry_add(
    registry_type="error_patterns",
    name="missing-import-pandas",
    description="pandas nicht installiert in Pipeline",
    content={
      "error_regex": "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'pandas'",
      "root_cause": "requirements-Feld im Step fehlt",
      "fix": "Step-YAML: requirements: [pandas]. Oder Pipeline-Level requirements.",
      "pipeline": "any",
      "severity": "low"
    },
    tags=["python", "import", "requirements"]
  )

## Workflow bei neuem Fehler

  1. get_run_errors(run_id=...) → Fehler-Text
  2. brix__registry_search(query=<fehler-schlüsselwort>, registry_types=["error_patterns"])
  3. Wenn gefunden: Fix anwenden
  4. Wenn nicht gefunden: diagnose_run → Lösung finden → registry_add (für nächstes Mal)

## Suche in Error-Patterns

  brix__registry_search(
    query="Connection refused",
    registry_types=["error_patterns"]
  )

help_topics.name: foreach
help_topics.title: Output-Struktur {items, summary}, results Shorthand, flat_output, selectattr/map Beispiele.
help_topics.content:
=== foreach — Parallel-Iteration über Listen ===

## Grundstruktur

  {
    "id": "process_items",
    "type": "python",
    "foreach": "{{ fetch_list.results }}",
    "concurrency": 5,
    "config": {
      "code": "result = process(item['name'])"
    }
  }

Regeln:
  • foreach muss eine Liste sein (Jinja2-Ausdruck)
  • concurrency MUSS ein int sein — KEIN Jinja2-Template!
  • item enthält das aktuelle Element
  • item.field für Felder des Elements
  • ACHTUNG: loop.index0 ist NICHT verfügbar zwischen foreach-Steps.
    Für Cross-Referenz: einen kombinierten Helper nutzen der alles pro Item macht.

## Output-Struktur eines foreach-Steps

  {
    "items": [                        ← Pro-Item Ergebnisse
      {"item": {...}, "output": ..., "success": true/false, "error": null},
      ...
    ],
    "summary": {                      ← Zusammenfassung
      "total": 10,
      "succeeded": 9,
      "failed": 1
    }
  }

## results-Shorthand (Jinja2)

  {{ process_items.results }}
  → Equivalent zu: {{ process_items.output.items }}
  → Gibt die pro-Item-Ergebnis-Liste zurück

  Für selectattr/map:
  {{ process_items.results | selectattr('success', 'eq', true) | list }}
  {{ process_items.results | map(attribute='output') | list }}
  {{ process_items.results | map(attribute='output.id') | list }}

  WICHTIG: results-Shorthand ist in Step-Params verfügbar aber NICHT im
  Pipeline output:-Feld. Im output:-Feld muss step_id.output.items verwendet werden.

## flat_output (flatten: true)

  Wenn jeder Item-Step eine Liste zurückgibt und du eine flache Liste willst:

  {
    "id": "extract_all",
    "type": "python",
    "foreach": "{{ files.output }}",
    "flatten": true,
    "config": {"code": "result = [line for line in open(item).readlines()]"}
  }

  → extract_all.output ist eine flache Liste aller Zeilen aller Dateien
  → OHNE flatten: Liste von Listen

## Anti-Patterns bei foreach

  ❌ base64 in foreach-Loops: große Dateien als base64 übergeben
     → Crash bei vielen Items (80 PDFs × 200KB = 16MB JSON)
     → FIX: Dateipfade als Strings übergeben, im Step öffnen

  ❌ concurrency: '{{ input.workers }}'
     → concurrency muss ein int-Literal sein
     → FIX: concurrency: 5 (direkt als Zahl)

  ❌ foreach: über leere Liste iterieren ohne | default([])
     → UndefinedError wenn vorheriger Step übersprungen wurde
     → FIX: foreach: "{{ prev.output.items | default([]) }}"

help_topics.name: lessons-learned
help_topics.title: Praxis-Erkenntnisse (persist teure Zwischenergebnisse, Credentials nie als Input, etc.).
help_topics.content:
=== Lessons Learned — Praxis-Erkenntnisse ===

## 1. Teure Zwischenergebnisse immer persistieren

  Problem: LLM-Batch über 100 Dokumente läuft 20 Minuten.
  Schritt 47 schlägt fehl → alles verloren → von vorn.

  Lösung: Nach jedem Chunk State speichern:
  sdk.state.set("batch_progress", {"done": processed, "total": total})

  Resume-Logik am Anfang:
  checkpoint = sdk.state.get("batch_progress") or {"done": 0}
  items_todo = all_items[checkpoint["done"]:]

## 2. Credentials NIEMALS als Input-Parameter

  Problem: brix__run_pipeline(params={"api_key": "sk-..."})
  → API-Key landet in Run-History, Logs, get_run_log Output

  Lösung: Credential-Store + {{ credentials.MY_KEY }} im Step

## 3. foreach concurrency sinnvoll wählen

  Zu hoch (20+): Rate-Limits, OOM bei großen Payloads
  Zu niedrig (1): Kein Vorteil von Parallelisierung

  Faustregel:
  • API-Calls mit Rate-Limit: 3-5
  • LLM-Calls: 2-4 (teuer, langsam)
  • Datei-Operationen lokal: 8-10
  • HTTP ohne Rate-Limit: 5-10

## 4. Datei-Pfade statt base64 übergeben

  Problem: 50 PDFs × 500KB base64 = 25MB JSON-Payload → OOM

  Lösung: {"path": "/host/root/docs/file.pdf", "name": "file.pdf"}
  Im Step: open(item["path"], "rb").read()

## 5. | default() für optionale Steps IMMER setzen

  Wenn ein Step mit when-Kondition übersprungen werden kann:
  {{ optional_step.output | default({}) }}
  {{ optional_step.output.items | default([]) }}

  Vergessen: UndefinedError bei jedem Skip

## 6. foreach-Ergebnisse auf Fehler prüfen

  Nicht einfach annehmen dass alle Items erfolgreich waren:

  succeeded = [r for r in classify.results if r["success"]]
  failed = [r for r in classify.results if not r["success"]]
  sdk.log(f"{len(failed)} Items fehlgeschlagen: {[r['error'] for r in failed]}")

## 7. Async-Runs für lange Pipelines

  brix__run_pipeline(name="long-job", async_mode=True)
  → Gibt sofort run_id zurück
  → Status pollen mit get_run_status

  Nicht auf synchronen Return warten bei >5 Minuten erwarteter Laufzeit.

## 8. Server-Health vor großen Runs prüfen

  brix__server_health(server="m365")
  → Prüft ob MCP-Server erreichbar ist BEVOR Pipeline startet

  Verhindert: Pipeline läuft 5 Minuten, dann erst MCP-Fehler

## 9. Pipeline-Namen konsistent halten

  Convention: kebab-case, beschreibend
  Gut: "buddy-extract-invoices", "fetch-outlook-inbox"
  Schlecht: "pipeline1", "test", "my_pipeline_v2_final"

## 10. Diagnose-Tools ZUERST nutzen

  Bei Fehler NICHT sofort in Code springen:
  1. get_run_errors → Was ist schiefgelaufen?
  2. diagnose_run → Warum?
  3. auto_fix_step → Kann Brix es selbst fixen?
  4. Erst dann: update_step mit Fix

help_topics.name: quick-start
help_topics.title: Ich bin neu. Workflow: get_tips → list_bricks → create_pipeline → run_pipeline. Minimales Beispiel.
help_topics.content:
=== Brix Quick-Start ===

Neu hier? Das ist der schnellste Weg zum ersten Pipeline-Run:

1. get_tips           → Überblick: Bricks, Pipelines, Konventionen
2. list_bricks        → Welche Bausteine gibt es?
3. create_pipeline    → Pipeline mit Steps inline erstellen
4. run_pipeline       → Pipeline ausführen, run_id zurück
5. get_run_status     → Status prüfen (running/completed/failed)
6. get_run_errors     → Bei Fehler: LLM-optimierte Fehleranalyse

Minimales Beispiel (HTTP-Fetch + Ausgabe):

  brix__create_pipeline(
    name="hello-world",
    steps=[
      {
        "id": "fetch",
        "type": "http",
        "config": {"url": "https://example.com/api/get", "method": "GET"}
      },
      {
        "id": "show",
        "type": "python",
        "config": {"code": "print(input_data)"},
        "input": "{{ fetch.output }}"
      }
    ]
  )

  brix__run_pipeline(name="hello-world")

Wichtigste Regeln:
  • KEIN YAML manuell schreiben → create_pipeline mit steps inline
  • KEIN brix run via Bash → brix__run_pipeline
  • KEIN Container-Rebuild → pipelines/ ist Volume-gemountet
  • Host-Pfade: /root/... → im Container /host/root/...

Weitere Topics: step-referenzen, foreach, debugging, anti-patterns

help_topics.name: registries
help_topics.title: 6 Wissens-Registries: templates, patterns, schemas, error_patterns, best_practices, lessons_learned. CRUD + Suche.
help_topics.content:
=== Registry-System — 6 Wissens-Registries (T-BRIX-V7-10) ===

Brix bietet 6 persistente Registries für strukturiertes Wissen. Alle Daten
liegen in brix.db und sind per MCP-Tools verwaltbar.

## Übersicht der Registries

  templates          — Pipeline-Templates (Steps-Pattern, Use Cases, Tags)
  patterns           — Wiederverwendbare Step-Patterns (YAML, Input/Output Schema)
  schemas            — Daten-Schemas (JSON Schema, Version, Beschreibung)
  error_patterns     — Bekannte Fehler-Patterns (Regex, Root Cause, Fix)
  best_practices     — Best Practices (Kategorie, Regel, Rationale, Beispiel)
  lessons_learned    — Lessons Learned (Kontext, Problem, Lösung, Impact)

## MCP-Tools

  brix__registry_add(registry_type, name, content, tags, description)
  brix__registry_get(registry_type, name_or_id)
  brix__registry_list(registry_type, tag_filter)
  brix__registry_update(registry_type, name_or_id, content, tags, description)
  brix__registry_delete(registry_type, name_or_id)
  brix__registry_search(query, registry_types)

## Beispiele

### Template hinzufügen
  brix__registry_add(
    registry_type="templates",
    name="m365-mail-processor",
    description="Mails fetchen, klassifizieren, verschieben",
    content={
      "steps_pattern": ["fetch", "classify", "move"],
      "use_cases": ["inbox-processing", "newsletter-cleanup"],
      "example_pipeline": "buddy-intake-outlook"
    },
    tags=["m365", "mail", "classify"]
  )

### Error-Pattern erfassen
  brix__registry_add(
    registry_type="error_patterns",
    name="mcp-connection-refused",
    description="MCP-Server nicht erreichbar",
    content={
      "error_regex": "Connection refused.*:808",
      "root_cause": "MCP-Container läuft nicht oder falscher Port",
      "fix": "docker compose up -d <service>; brix__server_health()",
      "pipeline": "any"
    },
    tags=["mcp", "connection", "infrastructure"]
  )

### Best Practice erfassen
  brix__registry_add(
    registry_type="best_practices",
    name="foreach-concurrency",
    description="Optimale Concurrency für foreach-Steps",
    content={
      "category": "performance",
      "rule": "API-Calls: 3-5, LLM-Calls: 2-4, lokale Dateien: 8-10",
      "rationale": "Zu hoch = Rate-Limits + OOM, zu niedrig = kein Benefit",
      "example": "foreach: ..., concurrency: 4"
    },
    tags=["foreach", "performance", "concurrency"]
  )

### Suche über alle Registries
  brix__registry_search(query="concurrency")
  → Findet Einträge in allen 6 Registries die 'concurrency' enthalten

### Gefilterte Liste
  brix__registry_list(registry_type="best_practices", tag_filter="performance")
  → Nur Best Practices mit Tag 'performance'

## Datenstruktur (alle Registries identisch)

  {
    "id": "uuid",
    "name": "eindeutiger-name",
    "description": "Kurzbeschreibung",
    "content": { ... },  // Beliebige JSON-Struktur
    "tags": ["tag1", "tag2"],
    "created_at": "ISO-Timestamp",
    "updated_at": "ISO-Timestamp"
  }

## Content-Konventionen pro Registry-Typ

  templates:       {steps_pattern, use_cases, example_pipeline, input_params}
  patterns:        {steps_yaml, input_schema, output_schema, description}
  schemas:         {json_schema, version, format}
  error_patterns:  {error_regex, root_cause, fix, pipeline, severity}
  best_practices:  {category, rule, rationale, example, anti_pattern}
  lessons_learned: {context, problem, solution, date, impact, tags}

help_topics.name: sdk
help_topics.title: brix.sdk Referenz komplett (mcp.call, progress, is_cancelled, state, lock, http).
help_topics.content:
=== brix.sdk — Vollständige Referenz ===

## Import

  import brix.sdk as sdk

## MCP-Tool aufrufen

  result = sdk.mcp.call(
      server="m365",
      tool="list-mail-messages",
      params={"folder": "Inbox", "top": 10}
  )
  # result ist das Tool-Output-Dict

  Async-Version:
  result = await sdk.mcp.call_async(server, tool, params)

## Fortschritt reporten

  sdk.progress(
      current=5,
      total=10,
      message="Verarbeite Dokument 5/10..."
  )
  # Erscheint in get_run_status und get_run_log

## Abbruch prüfen

  if sdk.is_cancelled():
      sdk.log("Run wurde abgebrochen — cleanup...")
      sys.exit(0)

  # Am besten in langen Loops:
  for item in items:
      if sdk.is_cancelled():
          break
      process(item)

## Logging

  sdk.log("Nachricht")              # Info-Level
  sdk.log("Warnung", level="warn")  # Warn-Level
  sdk.log("Fehler", level="error")  # Error-Level

## State lesen/schreiben (aus Helper heraus)

  sdk.state.set("my_key", {"data": [1, 2, 3]})
  value = sdk.state.get("my_key")
  sdk.state.delete("my_key")

## Ressourcen-Lock

  with sdk.lock("resource-name", timeout=30):
      # Exklusiver Zugriff — andere Helpers/Steps warten
      do_exclusive_work()

## HTTP-Calls (mit retry)

  response = sdk.http.get("https://api.example.com/data",
      headers={"Authorization": "Bearer " + credentials["API_KEY"]},
      retries=3,
      timeout=30
  )
  data = response.json()

help_topics.name: step-referenzen
help_topics.title: VOLLSTÄNDIG: {{ step_id.output }}, {{ input.x }}, {{ item }}, {{ credentials.X }}, foreach results, FALSCH-Patterns.
help_topics.content:
=== Step-Referenzen (Jinja2-Syntax) ===

## Output eines vorherigen Steps referenzieren

  {{ step_id.output }}           ✅  Ganzer Output des Steps (dict/string/list)
  {{ step_id.output.field }}     ✅  Einzelnes Feld aus dict-Output
  {{ step_id.output[0] }}        ✅  Erstes Element aus list-Output
  {{ step_id.output.nested.x }}  ✅  Verschachtelter Zugriff

## Pipeline-Input-Parameter

  {{ input.param_name }}         ✅  Input-Parameter der Pipeline
  {{ input.file_path }}          ✅  Typischer Datei-Pfad-Parameter
  {{ input.count | default(10) }} ✅  Mit Default-Wert

## foreach-Kontext

  {{ item }}                     ✅  Aktuelles Element in foreach-Loop
  {{ item.name }}                ✅  Feld des aktuellen Elements
  {{ item.id }}                  ✅  ID des aktuellen Elements

## Credentials (UUID-Referenz)

  {{ credentials.MY_CRED }}      ✅  Credential-Wert per Name
  {{ credentials.API_KEY }}      ✅  API-Key aus Credential-Store

## foreach-Ergebnisse (nach dem Loop)

  {{ loop_step.results }}        ✅  Alle Ergebnisse als Liste (shorthand)
  {{ loop_step.output.items }}   ✅  Items-Liste im Output-Dict
  {{ loop_step.output.summary }} ✅  Summary-Dict des foreach-Loops

  Selectattr/map Beispiel:
  {{ loop_step.results | selectattr('success', 'eq', true) | list }}
  {{ loop_step.results | map(attribute='output.id') | list }}

## FALSCH — Diese Patterns funktionieren NICHT

  {{ steps.step_id.data }}       ❌  FALSCH: kein 'steps.' Prefix
  {{ step_id.data }}             ❌  FALSCH: Feld heißt 'output', nicht 'data'
  {{ step_id.result }}           ❌  FALSCH: 'result' (singular) existiert nicht
  {{ step_id.results.output }}   ❌  FALSCH: 'results' nur bei foreach-Steps
  {{ context.step_id }}          ❌  FALSCH: kein 'context.' Prefix

## Pipeline output:-Feld — Eingeschränkter Kontext

  Das Pipeline output:-Template hat eingeschränkten Jinja2-Kontext.
  step_id.results funktioniert dort NICHT.

  ✅ RICHTIG im output:-Feld:
    output: "{{ my_step.output.summary }}"
    output: "{{ my_step.output['items'] }}"
    output: "{{ my_step.output }}"

  ❌ FALSCH im output:-Feld:
    output: "{{ my_step.results }}"   ← results-Shorthand nicht verfügbar hier

## Konditionaler Step (when)

  when: "{{ fetch.output.status == 'ok' }}"        ✅
  when: "{{ input.debug | default(false) }}"       ✅
  when: "{{ items | length > 0 }}"                 ✅
  when: "{{ fetch.output }}"                       ✅  (truthy check)

## default()-Filter (wichtig für optionale Steps)

  {{ maybe_step.output | default([]) }}   ← Step könnte übersprungen sein
  {{ input.limit | default(100) }}        ← Optionaler Parameter

help_topics.name: templates
help_topics.title: Pipeline-Vererbung mit extends.
help_topics.content:
=== Pipeline-Vererbung mit extends ===

## Konzept

Templates sind Basis-Pipelines die andere Pipelines ableiten können.
Gemeinsame Steps müssen nur einmal definiert werden.

## Template erstellen

  brix__create_pipeline(
    name="base-http-template",
    description="Basis für HTTP-Pipelines mit Auth",
    is_template=True,
    steps=[
      {
        "id": "auth",
        "type": "python",
        "config": {"code": "headers = {'Authorization': f'Bearer {credentials.API_KEY}'}"}
      }
    ]
  )

## Von Template ableiten

  brix__create_pipeline(
    name="my-api-pipeline",
    extends="base-http-template",
    steps=[
      {
        "id": "fetch_data",
        "type": "http",
        "config": {
          "url": "https://api.example.com/data",
          "headers": "{{ auth.output }}"
        }
      }
    ]
  )

## Template anzeigen

  brix__get_template(name="base-http-template")

## Vererbungsregeln

  • Abgeleitete Pipeline erbt alle Steps des Templates
  • Eigene Steps werden NACH den Template-Steps ausgeführt
  • Template-Steps können mit gleicher ID überschrieben werden
  • Templates selbst können nicht direkt ausgeführt werden

help_topics.name: tools
help_topics.title: Tool-Entscheidungsbaum: 'Ich will X → nutze Y'.
help_topics.content:
=== Tool-Entscheidungsbaum ===

"Ich will X → nutze Y"

## Pipeline erstellen/bearbeiten

  Neue Pipeline erstellen         → brix__create_pipeline
  Pipeline anzeigen               → brix__get_pipeline
  Pipeline suchen                 → brix__search_pipelines
  Alle Pipelines listen           → brix__list_pipelines
  Pipeline umbenennen             → brix__rename_pipeline
  Pipeline löschen                → brix__delete_pipeline
  Pipeline validieren             → brix__validate_pipeline
  Pipeline testen (dry-run)       → brix__test_pipeline

## Steps bearbeiten

  Step hinzufügen                 → brix__add_step
  Step anzeigen                   → brix__get_step
  Step ändern                     → brix__update_step
  Step entfernen                  → brix__remove_step
  Pipeline-Metadaten ändern       → brix__update_pipeline

## Pipeline ausführen

  Pipeline starten                → brix__run_pipeline
  Status prüfen                   → brix__get_run_status
  Fehler analysieren              → brix__get_run_errors
  Vollständiges Log               → brix__get_run_log
  Run abbrechen                   → brix__cancel_run
  Run suchen                      → brix__run_search
  Run annotieren                  → brix__run_annotate
  Run-Historie                    → brix__get_run_history
  Run löschen                     → brix__delete_run

## Debugging & Diagnose

  Fehler diagnostizieren          → brix__diagnose_run
  Auto-Fix anwenden               → brix__auto_fix_step
  Insights & Statistiken          → brix__get_insights
  Proaktive Vorschläge            → brix__get_proactive_suggestions

## Helper-Scripts

  Helper erstellen                → brix__create_helper
  Helper registrieren             → brix__register_helper
  Helpers listen                  → brix__list_helpers
  Helper anzeigen                 → brix__get_helper
  Helper suchen                   → brix__search_helpers
  Helper bearbeiten               → brix__update_helper
  Helper umbenennen               → brix__rename_helper
  Helper löschen                  → brix__delete_helper

## Bricks (Baustein-Typen)

  Alle Bricks listen              → brix__list_bricks
  Bricks suchen                   → brix__search_bricks
  Brick-Schema anzeigen           → brix__get_brick_schema

## Credentials

  Credential speichern            → brix__credential_add
  Alle Credentials listen         → brix__credential_list
  Credential abrufen              → brix__credential_get
  Credential aktualisieren        → brix__credential_update
  Credential rotieren             → brix__credential_rotate
  Credential suchen               → brix__credential_search
  Credential löschen              → brix__credential_delete

## Versionierung

  Versionen anzeigen              → brix__get_versions
  Versionen vergleichen           → brix__diff_versions
  Rollback                        → brix__rollback

## Alerts

  Alert erstellen                 → brix__alert_add
  Alerts listen                   → brix__alert_list
  Alert aktualisieren             → brix__alert_update
  Alert-Historie                  → brix__alert_history
  Alert löschen                   → brix__alert_delete

## Trigger & Scheduler

  Trigger erstellen               → brix__trigger_add
  Trigger listen                  → brix__trigger_list
  Trigger anzeigen                → brix__trigger_get
  Trigger aktualisieren           → brix__trigger_update
  Trigger testen                  → brix__trigger_test
  Trigger löschen                 → brix__trigger_delete
  Scheduler-Status                → brix__scheduler_status
  Scheduler starten               → brix__scheduler_start
  Scheduler stoppen               → brix__scheduler_stop

## Trigger-Gruppen

  Gruppe erstellen                → brix__trigger_group_add
  Gruppen listen                  → brix__trigger_group_list
  Gruppe starten                  → brix__trigger_group_start
  Gruppe stoppen                  → brix__trigger_group_stop
  Gruppe löschen                  → brix__trigger_group_delete

## MCP-Server

  Server hinzufügen               → brix__server_add
  Server listen                   → brix__server_list
  Server aktualisieren            → brix__server_update
  Server entfernen                → brix__server_remove
  Server-Status prüfen            → brix__server_health
  Server neu laden                → brix__server_refresh

## Agent State & Koordination

  Kontext speichern               → brix__save_agent_context
  Kontext wiederherstellen        → brix__restore_agent_context
  Ressource reservieren           → brix__claim_resource
  Ressource-Status prüfen         → brix__check_resource
  Ressource freigeben             → brix__release_resource
  State setzen                    → brix__state_set
  State abrufen                   → brix__state_get
  State listen                    → brix__state_list
  State löschen                   → brix__state_delete

## Orientierung

  Erste Schritte                  → brix__get_tips
  Hilfe zu einem Topic            → brix__get_help(topic="...")
  Pipeline-Template anzeigen      → brix__get_template

help_topics.name: triggers
help_topics.title: Trigger-System + Chaining + Scheduler.
help_topics.content:
=== Trigger-System + Chaining + Scheduler ===

## Trigger-Typen

  • schedule  — Cron-basiert oder Intervall
  • webhook   — HTTP-Endpoint
  • file      — Datei-Änderungen beobachten
  • event     — Interne Brix-Events

## Trigger erstellen

  brix__trigger_add(
    pipeline_name="my-pipeline",
    trigger_type="schedule",
    config={
      "cron": "0 9 * * 1-5",   # Mo-Fr 9 Uhr
      "timezone": "Europe/Berlin"
    }
  )

  Webhook-Trigger:
  brix__trigger_add(
    pipeline_name="my-pipeline",
    trigger_type="webhook",
    config={"secret": "my-secret", "method": "POST"}
  )

## Scheduler steuern

  brix__scheduler_start()     → Scheduler starten (lädt alle Triggers)
  brix__scheduler_stop()      → Scheduler stoppen
  brix__scheduler_status()    → Status + aktive Triggers

## Pipeline Chaining

  Am Ende einer Pipeline eine andere starten:

  {
    "id": "chain_next",
    "type": "mcp",
    "config": {
      "server": "brix",
      "tool": "run_pipeline",
      "params": {
        "name": "next-pipeline",
        "params": {"input_data": "{{ prev_step.output }}"}
      }
    }
  }

## Trigger-Gruppen

  Mehrere Trigger gemeinsam verwalten:

  brix__trigger_group_add(name="morning-jobs", trigger_ids=["t1", "t2", "t3"])
  brix__trigger_group_start(name="morning-jobs")   → Alle Triggers aktivieren
  brix__trigger_group_stop(name="morning-jobs")    → Alle deaktivieren

## Trigger testen

  brix__trigger_test(trigger_id="my-trigger", payload={"test": true})
  → Führt Pipeline einmalig aus als ob Trigger ausgelöst hätte
